Authors: José Gómez Torrecillas, F. J. Lobillo and Gabriel Navarro
Apellidos: Gómez Torrecillas, F. J. Lobillo and Gabriel Navarro
Title: A knapsack McEliece-based public key cryptosystem
abstract: In this talk, we describe a knapsack-type public key encryption scheme inspired by the McEliece cryptosystem and by the decoding algorithm of Goppa codes. The decryption process is efficiently carried out using a truncated version of the extended Euclidean algorithm. Moreover, the key generation and the encryption–decryption procedures exhibit remarkable time efficiency, as they rely exclusively on basic integer arithmetic. Since the plaintext alphabet is not necessarily binary, the security of the scheme relies on the hardness of a modular weighted subset sum problem, which is closely related to the classical NP-hard subset sum problem. We shall discuss several security aspects of the construction, focusing on lattice reduction attacks, which constitute the main class of attacks against this type of cryptosystem.

Authors: Federico Castillo Castillo, Daniel Duarte, Maximiliano Leyton-Alvarez and Alvaro Liendo
Apellidos: Castillo Castillo, Daniel Duarte, Maximiliano Leyton-Alvarez and Alvaro Liendo
Title: On a computational approach to the Nash blowup problem
abstract:
The Nash blowup was introduced as a potential method for resolving singularities. In the toric setting, this construction admits a purely combinatorial description in terms of affine semigroups, Hilbert bases, and polyhedral operations, revealing the problem to be fundamentally discrete.
We develop a computational framework to study iterative Nash blowups via these combinatorial structures. While extensive experiments provide strong evidence for termination in low dimensions, we unexpectedly discover explicit higher-dimensional toric counterexamples showing that iteration does not always yield a resolution.
Our results highlight subtle combinatorial obstructions and raise new structural and algorithmic questions at the interface of algebraic geometry and discrete mathematics.

Authors: María A. Hernández Cifre, Víctor Jiménez López and Mario Ruiz Amor
Apellidos: Hernández Cifre, Víctor Jiménez López and Mario Ruiz Amor
Title: On the Minkowski-Hlawka theorem
abstract: The well-known Minkowski-Hlawka theorem establishes that if $S\subset\mathbb{R}^n$, $n\geq 2$, is a $0$-symmetric star body whose volume is bounded from above by (twice) the Riemann zeta function, then there exists a $K$-admissible lattice with determinant $1$. This results has several equivalent versions due to its connection with both the Geometry of Numbers and the Packing Theory. Astonishingly, Minkowski-Hlawka's theorem in dimension $n=1$ is not known in the literature. In this work we prove this result in the different settings it can be stated: for $0$-symmetric star bodies, for star bodies not necessarily symmetric, and for arbitrary sets.

Authors: Sandor Ernesto Tuñón Andrés, Alejandro Estrada Moreno and Juan Alberto Rodríguez Velázquez
Apellidos: Tuñón Andrés, Alejandro Estrada Moreno and Juan Alberto Rodríguez Velázquez
Title: The Equidistant Dimension of Corona Product Graphs

Authors: Alberto Espuny Díaz, António Girão, Bertille Granet and Gal Kronenberg
Apellidos: Espuny Díaz, António Girão, Bertille Granet and Gal Kronenberg
Title: Sublinear degree conditions for 2-factors with k cycles in Hamiltonian graphs
abstract: Let $k\geq 2$.     We show that, for a sufficiently small $\varepsilon>0$, any sufficiently large $n$-vertex Hamiltonian graph of minimum degree at least $n^{1-\varepsilon}$ contains a $2$-factor consisting of exactly $k$ cycles.     This is the first minimum-degree condition for this problem which is polynomially smaller than linear.

Authors: Sara Asensio, Ignacio García-Marco and Philippe Gimenez
Apellidos: Asensio, Ignacio García-Marco and Philippe Gimenez
Title: On the special shape of some Betti diagrams of edge ideals
abstract:
Edge ideals of graphs were introduced by Villarreal in 1990, and have been the subject of many studies since then. In the same year, Fröberg characterized edge ideals with regularity 2 in combinatorial terms. This result was generalized by Fernández-Ramos and Gimenez to regularity 3 for bipartite graphs. A key ingredient in these results is the particular shape of the Betti diagrams of the edge ideals of the graphs obtained after removing a Hamiltonian cycle from either a complete graph $\mathcal K_k$ or a complete bipartite graph $\mathcal K_{k,k}$.
In this work, we consider the family of Generalized Andrásfai graphs ${\rm GA}(t,k)$ with $t,k \geq 2$. This family extends the family of complete bipartite graphs, since $\mathcal K_{k,k} = {\rm GA}(2,k)$. We show that the results known for $\mathcal K_k$ and $\mathcal K_{k,k}$ can be naturally extended to this family. More precisely, when removing a suitable Hamiltonian cycle from ${\rm GA}(t,k)$, the resulting edge ideal has regularity $t+2$, projective dimension $t(k-2)$ and a Betti diagram exhibiting a generalized version of the same special shape. Our proofs combine combinatorial, homological and algebraic tools that have played an important role in the study of edge ideals.

Authors: Francisco Javier Soto Sánchez
Apellidos: Soto Sánchez
Title: Local directed structure of algebraic Cayley digraphs over finite fields
abstract: We study Cayley digraphs on the multiplicative group of a finite field extension generated by values of monic polynomials of a fixed degree. A simple degree argument rules out short closed directed walks and yields a lower bound for the directed girth. For irreducible generators, we describe the directed ball around a vertex up to the natural degree threshold, derive closed formulas for local growth, and identify a canonical graded induced subdigraph that is directed acyclic in the same range.

Authors: Ignacio García-Marco, Philippe Gimenez and Mario González-Sánchez
Apellidos: García-Marco, Philippe Gimenez and Mario González-Sánchez
Title: From sumsets to Castelnuvo-Mumford regularity of toric varieties
abstract:
For a finite set $\mathcal{A} \subset \mathbb{N}^d$ and $s \in \mathbb{N}$, let $s\mathcal{A}$ denote its $s$-fold sumset. Although these sumsets may behave irregularly for small values of $s$, they eventually stabilize in a precise sense. This leads to the notion of sumsets regularity, denoted by $\sigma(\mathcal{A})$, defined as the first stage at which this stabilization occurs. In the first part of this work, we establish upper bounds for $\sigma(\mathcal{A})$ for certain families of sets $\mathcal{A} \subset \mathbb{N}^d$.
In the second part, we show that this combinatorial invariant controls the Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity of simplicial projective toric varieties with at most one singular point. More precisely, we prove that the regularity of such a variety is at most one more than the sumsets regularity of an associated set $\mathcal{A}$, which moreover belongs to one of the families studied in the first part. This yields explicit upper bounds on Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity. In particular, in the smooth case we recover the bound of Herzog and Hibi [Proc. Amer. Math. Soc. 131 (2003), 2641--2647], and hence the Eisenbud--Goto bound. Moreover, if the variety has exactly one singular point and its dimension is at least $3$, we prove that its regularity also satisfies the Eisenbud--Goto bound.
The link between sumsets regularity and Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity is provided by a Hochster-like formula that expresses the latter in terms of the simplicial homology of complexes naturally associated with the sumsets of~$\mathcal{A}$. The results presented in this work are part of the preprint

Authors: Danai Deligeorgaki and Krishna Menon
Apellidos: Deligeorgaki and Krishna Menon
Title: How to bounce your canon permutation
abstract:
We study a new class of palindromic descent polynomials. Given a Dyck path $d$ of semilength $n$ and a permutation $\sigma$ of size $n$, one can label the up-steps and down-steps of $d$ with the elements of $\sigma$. The labeled Dyck path determines a multiset permutation called a {canon} (or nonnesting) permutation. Such permutations also arise as linear extensions of posets and as regions of hyperplane arrangements. Elizalde showed that the descent polynomial for all canon permutations of fixed length factors as a product of an Eulerian and a Narayana polynomial.
We refine these polynomials by associating to $d$ a descent polynomial $C_d$ over the canon permutations obtained from $d$. We prove that $C_d$ is palindromic and free of internal zero coefficients, though not unimodal in general. Its degree is determined by the number of peaks in the bounce path of $d$. We establish a correspondence between canon permutations attaining the maximum number of descents and Dyck paths below $d$ in the Dyck lattice satisfying a valley condition. Each such path contributes a number of maximizers equal to the number of linear extensions of an associated poset, yielding a combinatorial interpretation of the leading coefficient of $C_d$.

Authors: Ruben Rodriguez and Umberto Martinez
Apellidos: Rodriguez and Umberto Martinez
Title: On the structure of Polynomial Ideal Codes and Nonlinear Reed--Solomon Codes.
abstract: We study polynomial ideal codes and nonlinear Reed--Solomon codes in the folded Hamming metric. We compute the maximal length of polynomial ideal codes, determine their duals as generalized polynomial ideal codes over the base field,  deduce that they are dually quasi-MDS and we obtain their weight-distribution. We then characterize when a nonlinear Reed--Solomon code is equivalent to a polynomial ideal code, namely when its evaluation points lie in pairwise distinct Frobenius conjugacy classes of full size. As consequences, we obtain duality and weight-distribution results for this family of nonlinear Reed--Solomon codes.

Authors: Lidia Gordo Malagón and María A. Hernández Cifre
Apellidos: Gordo Malagón and María A. Hernández Cifre
Title: On successive minima and successive radii
abstract: We establish new inequalities relating products of the successive minima of a $0$-symmetric convex body to the product of its successive outer and inner radii. These inequalities are motivated by results of Henk and Hernández, but relate only the sequences of successive minima and radii, without involving the volume.

Authors: Sylwia Antoniuk, Alberto Espuny Díaz, Kalina Petrova and Miloš Stojaković
Apellidos: Antoniuk, Alberto Espuny Díaz, Kalina Petrova and Miloš Stojaković
Title: Efficient construction of small subgraphs in the random graph process
abstract:
Consider the budget-constrained random graph process introduced by Frieze, Krivelevich and Michaeli, where each time an edge is offered through the (standard) random graph process we must irrevocably decide whether to "purchase" this edge or not, with our goal being to construct a graph which satisfies some property within a given time $t$ and while purchasing at most $b$ edges. We consider the problem of constructing graphs containing certain fixed small subgraphs.
We provide an optimal strategy for building a graph which contains a copy of $K_4$, showing that budget $b=\omega(\max\{n^8/t^5,n^2/t\})$ suffices and that if $b=o(\max\{n^8/t^5,n^2/t\})$ then no strategy can a.a.s.\ produce a graph containing a copy of $K_4$. This resolves a problem raised by Iľkovič, León and Shu. More generally, we obtain analogously tight results for containing a wheel of any fixed size, or a graph consisting of a tree plus one additional universal vertex. We also tackle the problem of constructing graphs containing a copy of $K_5$, obtaining both lower and upper bounds on the optimal budget, though a gap remains in this case.

Authors: Antonio Jesús Lorite Lopez, Daniel Camazon Portela and Juan Antonio Lopez Ramos
Apellidos: Lorite Lopez, Daniel Camazon Portela and Juan Antonio Lopez Ramos
Title: A Geometric Approach to Codes from Simplicial Complexes over $\mathbb{F}_2$
abstract:
Since their introduction, linear codes arising from simplicial complexes have been extensively studied from an algebraic perspective. In this work, however, we adopt a different approach: we analyze the parameters of these codes over $\mathbb{F}_2$ through the geometry of the simplicial complex that generates it, by studying how certain operations on the complex affect these parameters. This is achieved through a geometric interpretation of the weight of codewords, which allows us to describe how certain operations on the complex affect the code.
We also include tables of computed examples that illustrate the theoretical results and provide insight into how the code parameters can be manipulated via the operations introduced.

Authors: Luis Gustavo Da Soledade Gonzaga and Christiane Neme Campos
Apellidos: Gustavo Da Soledade Gonzaga and Christiane Neme Campos
Title: On improper colourings of apart-planar graphs
abstract: A $(k,d)$-colouring of a graph $G$ is a vertex colouring with at most $k$ colours such that every monochromatic connected component has maximum degree at most $d$.    Every planar graph admits $(3,2)$-colourings, but no $d$ exists for which every planar graph admits $(1,d)$- or $(2,d)$-colourings.   Whilst deciding whether a planar graph admits a $(2,1)$-colouring is NP-complete,   determining $(2,1)$-colourings for outerplanar graphs is polynomial.   In this work, we prove that deciding the existence of $(2,1)$-colourings of planar graphs in which every pair of triangles is disjoint remains NP-complete.

Authors: Marta Pavelka and Bruno Benedetti
Apellidos: Pavelka and Bruno Benedetti
Title: Line-Hamiltonian Polytopes
abstract: For $d \ge 2$, we show that all graphs of $d$-polytopes have a Hamiltonian line graph if and only if $d \ne 3$: We exhibit a graph of a $3$-polytope on $252$ vertices whose line graph does not even have Hamiltonian paths. Adapting a construction by Grünbaum and Motzkin, for large $n$ we also construct simple $3$-polytopes on $3n$ vertices in whose line graph any simple path is shorter than $10 n^{\alpha}$, for some constant $\alpha<1$.

Authors: Linxuan Li
Apellidos: Li
Title: Fans Arising from Lattice Polytopes of Mixed Volume One and Their Generalizations
abstract: Inspired by the Esterov--Gusev classification theorem for tuples of lattice polytopes of mixed volume one, we study a family of weighted balanced polyhedral fans canonically induced by their subtuples. We show that these fans admit a rigid combinatorial structure and obtain a finiteness classification in a distinguished low-dimensional case. Motivated also by Fink's classification of Bergman fans, we further generalize the construction to arbitrary weighted balanced fans and prove that the same type of structural and finiteness phenomena persists beyond the mixed-volume-one setting.

Authors: Monica Blanco, Francisco Criado and Francisco Santos
Apellidos: Blanco, Francisco Criado and Francisco Santos
Title: Coloopless Zonotopes and Counterexamples to the Shifted Lonely Runner Conjecture
abstract:
Starting with the reformulation of the Lonely Runner Conjecture (and its shifted version) in terms of "lonely runner zonotopes" (Henze and Malikiosis, 2017), in this work we do two things:
- We show that LR zonotopes fit into a much larger class of zonotopes, that we call coloopless. This class is quite general and closed under projection, which allows us to give a more general and transparent proof of the main result of Malikiosis-Santos-Schymura (2025): the smallest counter-examples to LRC, in case they exist, have sum of velocities bounded by $\binom{n+1}{2}^{n-1}$.
- We show counter-examples to the Shifted Lonely Runner Conjecture starting at $n=5$.

Authors: Kerri Morgan and Lluis Vena
Apellidos: Morgan and Lluis Vena
Title: On the chromatic unicity of wheels: characterizing all the cases
abstract: We characterize the remaining cases of the chromatic unicity of the wheel graphs. More precisely, we show that, if the wheel (a cycle graph with an additional vertex adjacent to everything else) has an even number of vertices and it is $\geq 10$, then such wheel is determined by its chromatic polynomial. This solves the remaining cases of a question by Chao and Whitehead from 1978 regarding the unicity of the chromatic polynomials of the wheel graphs. We use an extension of Whitney/Farrel's work to express the coefficients of the chromatic polynomials as expressions depending on the induced subgraphs.  We use these results to conclude that any graph chromatically equivalent to the wheel should contain a complete graph on $4$ vertices as a subgraph. Then, we combine such conclusion with how the chromatic polynomial  depends on (some) acyclic orientations which was given by Bernardi and Nadeau to show that the wheels with more than $9$ vertices are chromatically unique.

Authors: Hoory Shlomo and Daniel Kotlar
Apellidos: Shlomo and Daniel Kotlar
Title: On the equal sum partition problem
abstract:
We consider the equal sum partition problem, motivated by distance magic graph labeling: Given $n,k \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $k\, | \sum_{i=1}^ni$ and a partition $p_1+\cdots+p_k=n$, when is it possible to find a partition of the set $\{1,2,\ldots,n\}$ into $k$ subsets of sizes $p_1,\dots,p_k$, such that the element sum in each subset is the same?
A well-known necessary condition is the slack condition, which requires that, for all $j$, placing the largest possible elements in the $j$ smallest sets yields a total sum at least as large as needed. However, this condition is not sufficient, and known counterexamples exist.
In this work, we extend the list of unsolvable problem instances satisfying the slack condition by exhibiting infinite families where the $n/k$ ratio is any rational number in the interval $(2,\frac{24}{7})$. Furthermore, we show that the slack condition is natural, as it is both necessary and sufficient for the fractional relaxation of the problem. Guided by this observation, we prove that the problem is solvable with high probability for the class of linear partitions, where $k$ is fixed, $p_1,\ldots,p_k$ grow linearly with $n$, and where the slack condition holds in a strong sense. We do this by applying a randomized rounding algorithm to a solution of the fractional relaxation of the problem.

Authors: Jack Allsop and Patrick Morris
Apellidos: Allsop and Patrick Morris
Title: Substructures in random Latin squares
abstract: This abstract studies the probability of substructures occurring in random Latin squares. A consequence of our main result is that for a partial Latin square $P$ of order $n$ with $o(n)$ non-empty rows and columns (as $n \to \infty$) and $k$ total non-empty cells and a random Latin square $\mathbf{L}$ of order $n$, the probability that $\mathbf{L}$ contains $P$ lies between $((1/23-o(1))/n)^k$ and $((23+o(1))/n)^k$. We apply this result to subsquares in random Latin squares to obtain the first proof of the fact that the expected number of subsquares of order $3$ in a random Latin square of order $n$ is non-vanishing as $n \to \infty$. We are also able to provide the best known asymptotics for the expected number of subsquares of order $m$ in a random Latin square of order $n$ when $m=o(n)$.

Authors: Gergely Kiss
Apellidos: Kiss
Title: Cylinder type and $p$-divisible sets in $\mathbb{F}_p^3$
abstract: A set $S\subseteq \mathbb{F}_p^n$ is called $p$-divisible if every affine hyperplane meets $S$ in $0 \pmod p$ points. Ball's strong cylinder conjecture predicts that every $p$-divisible set of size $p^2$ in $\mathbb{F}_p^3$ is a cylinder. We prove a structural relaxation of this statement: every $p$-divisible multiset on $\mathbb{F}_p^3$ lies in the $\mathbb{F}_p$-linear span of cylinders, equivalently in the span of differences of parallel lines, and every nonnegative integer-valued $p$-divisible multiset of total weight $p^2$ is in fact a $\mathbb{Z}$-linear combination of cylinders. The proof is based on an explicit polynomial description of the orthogonal complement of the span of affine planes. This isolates a natural remaining rigidity question toward Ball's conjecture.

Authors: Hoi Ping Luk, Roman Nedela and Christopher Purcell
Apellidos: Luk, Roman Nedela and Christopher Purcell
Title: Tiling the sphere with regular polygons
abstract: We give a complete classification of edge-to-edge tilings of the sphere by regular polygons under a unified framework. Our proof does not assume local convexity of the tiles, nor global symmetry of the tilings, nor polyhedrality of their underlying graphs. It is also independent of the Johnson-Zalgaller classification of solids with regular faces (1967) which took over $200$ pages. We apply a blend of trigonometric, algebraic and combinatorial tools of independent interest.

Authors: Egor Bakaev and Amir Yehudayoff
Apellidos: Bakaev and Amir Yehudayoff
Title: A note on the affine plank conjecture
abstract: In 1951, Bang posed the affine plank conjecture, which remains open: If a convex body in $\mathbb{R}^d$ is covered by planks, then the total relative width of the planks is at least one. We prove a lower bound of $2/(1+\sqrt{d})$ for this total relative width. The best previously known lower bound was $2/(1+d)$.

Authors: Gabriela Araujo-Pardo and Leonardo Martinez-Sandoval
Apellidos: Araujo-Pardo and Leonardo Martinez-Sandoval
Title: On the arc chromatic number for Galois projective planes, affine planes and Euclidean grids. The fractional version for projective planes.
abstract:
Given any finite plane, defined as pairs $(\mathcal{P},\mathcal{L})$, where $\mathcal{P}$ and $\mathcal{L}$ represent the set of points and lines, respectively. An arc is a set of $\mathcal{P}$ containing no three collinear points.
In this work, we determine that the minimal number of arcs required to partition the Galois projective plane $PG(2,q)$ is $q+1$, and we also establish the exact value for a fractional variant of this problem. Extending our study to affine planes $AG(2,q)$, we prove that they admit partitions into $q$ arcs.
In particular, we show that this partition is tight when $q$ is an odd prime power, and that a $(q-1)$-partition is achievable for $q=2^k$ with $k \in \{1,2,3\}$. For $q=2^k$ with $k \ge 4$, we provide explicit bounds between the two feasible values.
Finally, we apply these results to Euclidean grids, establishing the existence of partitions into $(1+\varepsilon)n$ sets in general position for any $\varepsilon>0$ and sufficiently large $n$, and we give exact minimal partitions for small grids.

Authors: Cyril Banderier and Michael Drmota
Apellidos: Banderier and Michael Drmota
Title: Combinatorics and Asymptotics of Systems of Linear Catalytic Equations
abstract:
We provide a complete combinatorial and asymptotic analysis of positive linear systems of equations in one catalytic variable that appear in several combinatorial problems, such as lattice-path counting or stack-sortable permutation counting.
We show that the corresponding generating functions satisfy a positive polynomial system of equations (which is associated to a context-free grammar). Furthermore we prove a universal asymptotic behaviour.

Authors: Jean Cardinal and Micha Sharir
Apellidos: Cardinal and Micha Sharir
Title: Implicit representations via the polynomial method
abstract:
Semialgebraic graphs are graphs whose vertices are points in $\mathbb{R}^d$, and adjacency between two vertices is determined by the truth value of a semialgebraic predicate of constant complexity. We show how to harness polynomial partitioning methods to construct compact adjacency labeling schemes for families of semialgebraic graphs.
That is, we show that for any family of semialgebraic graphs, given a graph on $n$ vertices in this family, we can assign a label consisting of $O(n^{1-2/(d+1) + \varepsilon})$ bits to each vertex (where $\varepsilon > 0$ can be made arbitrarily small and the constant of proportionality depends on $\varepsilon$ and on the complexity of the adjacency-defining predicate), such that adjacency between two vertices can be determined solely from their two labels, without any additional information. We obtain for instance that unit disk graphs and segment intersection graphs have such labelings with labels of $O(n^{1/3 + \varepsilon})$ bits. This is in contrast to their natural implicit representation consisting of the coordinates of the disk centers or segment endpoints, which sometimes require exponentially many bits. It also improves on the best known bound of $O(n^{1-1/d}\log n)$ for $d$-dimensional semialgebraic families due to Alon (\textit{Discrete Comput. Geom.}, 2024), a bound that holds more generally for graphs with shattering functions bounded by a degree-$d$ polynomial in $n$. Our labeling scheme is efficient in the sense that not only adjacency between two vertices can be decided in time linear in the size of their labels, but the labels can be computed in subquadratic time on a real RAM from the input points and the semialgebraic adjacency predicate, using recent polynomial partitioning algorithms.
We also give new bounds on the size of adjacency labels for other families of graphs. In particular, we consider semilinear graphs, which are semialgebraic graphs in which the predicate only involves linear polynomials. We show that semilinear graphs have adjacency labels of size $O(\log n)$. We also prove that polygon visibility graphs, which are not semialgebraic in the above sense, have adjacency labels of size $O(\log^3 n)$.

Authors: Michal Černý, Miroslav Rada and Ondřej Sokol
Apellidos: Černý, Miroslav Rada and Ondřej Sokol
Title: Complexity of MaxVar and cliques in a random intersection graph
abstract: The MaxVar problem reads: given a family of $n$ compact intervals, select one number in each interval so as to maximize the variance of the selected numbers. The problem is NP-hard, but under a natural probabilistic model it is solvable in time $O(n^{1\, +\, 0.7/\log\log n})$ on average. This incredibly good bound is achieved by the algorithm by Ferson, Ginsburg, Kreinovich, Longpré and Aviles ("FGKLA") from DOI:~10.1007/s11155-005-3616-1. Its complexity depends exponentially on~$\omega_n$, the size of the largest clique in the intersection graph of the intervals shrunk by factor~$n$. We focus on the case when the intervals have infinite expected width, complementing the finite case analyzed in DOI:~10.1007/s00037-024-00254-3. (In a sense, this makes the picture of behavior of FGKLA complete.) We show that $\mathsf{E}\omega_n$, and thus the expected complexity of the algorithm, is driven by the speed of divergence of the integral of the survival function. If it diverges at the logarithmic rate, $\omega_n$ is still small enough and FGKLA works polynomially on average. (This property might seem surprising: one would guess $\omega_n$ to be large when the intervals have width $\infty$ on average.) We also derive lower bounds implying that above $\log n$ the algorithm becomes superpolynomial. For example: when the order of the highest finite moment is strictly below one (and bounded away from one), the algorithm needs time $\Omega(2^{n^{\varepsilon}})$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$. Finally, in addition to expected complexity, the proof method also allows us to derive bounds on smoothed complexity in the Spielman-Teng sense.

Authors: Víctor Franco Sánchez, Mercé Mora and María Luz Puertas
Apellidos: Franco Sánchez, Mercé Mora and María Luz Puertas
Title: Metric representations of graphs: minimality and computational complexity
abstract: The metric coordinates of a vertex $u$ of a graph $G$ with respect to an ordered set $W$ of vertices of $G$ is the vector of distances from $u$ to the vertices of $W$. We say that $W$ is a resolving set of $G$ if different vertices have different metric coordinates. Hence, if $W$ is a resolving set of a graph $G$, then the set of metric coordinates has cardinality the order of $G$. Here, we study the reverse problem, that is, given a set $S$ of vectors with integer entries, determine if there exists a graph $G$ and a resolving set $W$ of vertices of $G$ such that $S$ is the set of metric coordinates of the vertices of G with respect to $W$. In such a case, we say that the pair $(G,W)$ realizes $S$. The sets that can be realized in this way were characterized in a previous work. In this work, we study minimal realizations with respect to the inclusion of the set of edges and some computational complexity problems.

Authors: Alexey Gordeev
Apellidos: Gordeev
Title: Bollobás–Meir TSP Conjecture Holds Asymptotically
abstract:
In 1992, Bollobás and Meir showed that for every $k \geq 1$ there exists a constant $c_k$ such that, for any $n$ points in the $k$-dimensional unit cube $[0, 1]^k$, one can find a tour $x_1, \dots, x_n$ through these $n$ points with $\sum_{i = 1}^n |x_i - x_{i + 1}|^k \leq c_k$, where $x_{n + 1} = x_1$ and $|x - y|$ is the Euclidean distance between $x$ and $y$. Remarkably, this bound does not depend on $n$, the number of points. They conjectured that the optimal constant is $c_k = 2 \cdot k^{k / 2}$ and showed that it cannot be taken lower than that. This conjecture was recently revised for $k = 3$ by Balogh, Clemen and Dumitrescu, who showed that $c_3 \geq 2^{7/2} > 2 \cdot 3^{3/2}$. It remains open for all $k > 2$, with the best known upper bound $c_k \leq 2.65^k \cdot k^{k / 2} \cdot (1 + o_k(1))$.
We significantly narrow the gap between lower and upper bounds on $c_k$, reducing it from exponential to linear. Specifically, we prove that $c_k \leq 2\mathrm{e}(k + 1) \cdot k^{k / 2}$ and $c_k = k^{k / 2} \cdot (2 + o_k(1))$, the latter establishing the conjecture asymptotically. We also obtain analogous results for related problems on Hamiltonian paths, spanning trees and perfect matchings in the unit cube. Our main tool is a new generalization of the ball packing argument used in earlier works.

Authors: Pablo Candela, Diego González-Sánchez and Balázs Szegedy
Apellidos: Candela, Diego González-Sánchez and Balázs Szegedy
Title: An algorithmic spectral approach to higher-order Fourier analysis
abstract: We present a new approach to higher-order Fourier analysis on arbitrary finite abelian groups, based on spectral decompositions of certain Hermitian matrices. In particular, this yields a new regularity decomposition for functions relative to the Gowers $U^3$-norm, which can be computed efficiently (we will present implementations using Python). This extended abstract outlines the central ideas and results underpinning this approach, based partly on the preprint arXiv:2501.12287 and on papers in preparation.

Authors: Cameron Strachan and Konrad Swanepoel
Apellidos: Strachan and Konrad Swanepoel
Title: The number of occurrences of the two smallest distances
abstract: For each value of $\rho > 1$, let $e(n,\rho)$ denote the maximum combined number of occurrences of the smallest and second smallest distances in a set of $n$ points in the plane, given that the smallest distance is $1$ and the second smallest distance is $\rho$. We determine the asymptotics of $e(n,\rho) = f(\rho)n-o(n)$ for all except six values of $\rho$. For these exceptional values, we determine upper and lower bounds that are quite close. The hardest exceptional value is the golden ratio $\varphi=\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}$, where we give the bounds $53/13\leq f(\varphi)\leq 4.375$ with the lower bound disproving a conjecture of Csizmadia. It turns out that for all except finitely many values of $\rho$, $f(\rho)\in\{3,7/2\}$.

Authors: Simeon Ball and Robin Simoens
Apellidos: Ball and Robin Simoens
Title: Mutually entangled quantum Latin squares
abstract: We compare two ways to define the quantum analogue of mutually orthogonal Latin squares: with and without entanglement. We prove that there exist arbitrarily many mutually entangled quantum Latin squares (MEQLS) of any order, providing a motivation to look at non-entangled mutually orthogonal Latin squares (MOQLS) instead.

Authors: John Bamberg and Lukas Klawuhn
Apellidos: Bamberg and Lukas Klawuhn
Title: Designs of perfect matchings
abstract: We investigate generalisations of 1-factorisations and hyperfactorisations of the complete graph $K_{2n}$. We show that they are special subsets of the association scheme obtained from the Gelfand pair $(S_{2n},S_2 \wr S_n)$. This unifies and extends results by Cameron (1976) and gives rise to new existence and non-existence results. Our methods involve working in the group algebra $\mathbb{C}[S_{2n}]$ and using the representation theory of $S_{2n}$.

Authors: Tássio Naia, Guillem Perarnau and Xavier Povill
Apellidos: Naia, Guillem Perarnau and Xavier Povill
Title: Balanced subtournaments of a random tournament
abstract: We study the size of the largest balanced subtournament in a random tournament on $n$ vertices. A tournament is balanced if all vertices have equal indegree and outdegree. We show that, with high probability, the largest balanced subtournament has size $O(n^{2/3})$. Moreover, for every $k = o(n^{2/3})$, with high probability the random tournament on $n$ vertices contains a balanced subtournament of size $k$. Our proof combines enumeration results for tournaments with prescribed degree sequences, due to McKay and Wang, with probabilistic arguments of Krivelevich, Sudakov and Wormald.

Authors: Max Kölbl
Apellidos: Kölbl
Title: Toward a lattice interpretation of the roots of Ehrhart polynomials
abstract: Ehrhart polynomial roots have been an active object of study for the last 26 years, usually with regards to their distribution in $\mathbb{C}$. However, their meaning has remained mysterious. We present an approach in which an Ehrhart polynomial root, up to algebraic conjugates, in terms of the underlying lattice polytope by identifying it with a $p$-adic root arising from the same minimal polynomial.

Authors: R. Beltrán, J.M. Díaz-Báñez, J.M. Higes, A.J. Lozano, M.A. Pérez-Cutiño and I. Ventura
Apellidos: Beltrán, J.M. Díaz-Báñez, J.M. Higes, A.J. Lozano, M.A. Pérez-Cutiño and I. Ventura
Title: The shortest marsupial path in a simple polygon
abstract: Let $P$ be a simple polygon with $n$ vertices. Given two points $p$ and $q$ in $P$, we introduce the marsupial path from $p$ to $q$ as the concatenation of a geodesic path from $p$ to a point $\hat{a}$ and a line segment connecting $\hat{a}$ and $q$, where both subpaths are subject to length constraints. If such a path exists, we say that $p$ goes to $q$ marsupially. We analyze the geometric properties of these paths and present an $O(n)$-time algorithm to compute the shortest marsupial path connecting two given points, provided one exists. Moreover, we describe the marsupial visibility region of a point $p$, that is, the locus of points in $P$ to which $p$ can go marsupially.

Authors: Arnau Messegué and Josep M. Miret
Apellidos: Messegué and Josep M. Miret
Title: There do not exist almost Moore digraphs of odd prime diameter
abstract:
Almost Moore digraphs are diregular digraphs of degree $d>1$, diameter $k>1$, and order $N=d+d^2+\cdots +d^k$, that is, one unit less than the Moore bound. So far, their existence has only been proven for diameter $k=2$. It is also known that they do not exist for $k=3,4$ and $d=2,3$ if $k>2$. Furthermore, it is believed that they do not exist for any $k>2$.
In this extended abstract, we show that there is an infinite family of values $k$ such that there do not exist almost Moore digraphs of diameter $k$ for any $d\geq 4$. More precisely, we show that when $k$ is an odd prime, there do not exist almost Moore digraphs for any $d \geq 4$. Our results substantially extend the previously known nonexistence cases.

Authors: Emmanuel Briand
Apellidos: Messegué and Josep M. Miret
Title: Tridimensional rook placements and partial Latin rectangles through multisymmetric functions (extended abstract)
abstract:
We derive formulas for the generating series of the numbers of non-attacking rook placements in a tridimensional chessboard with 2 or 3 levels. These series count also partial Latin rectangles with $2$ or $3$ rows. Formulas for the corresponding rook polynomials are obtained as well.
The results are obtained following methods developed by MacMahon and Gessel for counting Latin squares and Latin rectangles, by means of scalar products of multisymmetric functions.

Authors: Tabriz Popatia, Simeon Ball and Michel Lavrauw
Apellidos: Popatia, Simeon Ball and Michel Lavrauw
Title: Additive codes, finite geometry, and Griesmer-type bounds
abstract:
Additive codes over finite fields admit a natural geometric description in terms of multisets of subspaces in projective space. In particular, an additive $[n,r/h,d]^h_q$ code is equivalent to a projective $h$-$(n,r,d)_q$ system, namely a multiset of $n$ subspaces of $\mathrm{PG}(r-1,q)$ of dimension at most $h-1$ such that each hyperplane contains at most $n-d$ of them, and some hyperplane contains exactly $n-d$. This correspondence allows questions on additive codes to be translated into finite-geometric problems.
In this talk we survey recent joint work on additive codes from this geometric viewpoint. We first discuss Griesmer-type bounds on the length of additive codes over finite fields, comparing them with the classical Griesmer bound for linear codes and with consequences for additive MDS codes. We then turn to constructions. In particular, we describe constructions of additive codes from linear codes using generalized Hamming weights and partial semifields, as well as a family of additive codes obtained from projective $h$-systems. These examples illustrate both the geometric nature of the theory and the sharpness of the bounds in certain parameter ranges. We conclude by highlighting families of additive codes with especially strong parameters, including examples meeting the additive Griesmer bound and examples outperforming the best known linear codes.

Authors: Gyula Károlyi
Apellidos: Károlyi
Title: On set addition with restricted restrictions
abstract: Let $A_1,A_2,\dots,A_n$ be subsets of a field and $\Gamma$ a graph with vertex set $V(\Gamma)=\{1,2,\dots,n\}$. Denote by $\sum_\Gamma A_i$ the set of all elements that can be written in the form $a_1+\dots+a_n$, where $a_i\in A_i$ and $a_i\ne a_j$ for $ij\in E(\Gamma)$. Extending a recent result of Wang and Sun, we provide lower bounds on $|\sum_\Gamma A_i|$ in the case when $\Gamma$ is either a path or a cycle, and the cardinalities of the sets $A_i$ are equal. Our proof depends on a quantitative variant of the Combinatorial Nullstellensatz known as the coefficient lemma. A generalization to powers of paths allows to form an idea about the transition from unrestricted to restricted set addition.

Authors: Gabriela Araujo-Pardo, Narda Cordero-Michel, Diego Gonzalez and Carmen Hernando
Apellidos: Araujo-Pardo, Narda Cordero-Michel, Diego Gonzalez and Carmen Hernando
Title: On locating and neighbor-locating colorings of Moore cages
abstract: A $k$-coloring of a graph $G$ is a $k$-partition $\Pi = \{S_1,..., S_k\}$ of $V(G)$ into independent sets, called colors. A $k$-coloring is called neighbor-locating} if for every pair of vertices $\{u,v\}$ belonging to the same color $S_i$, the set of colors of the neighborhood of $u$ is different from the set of colors of the neighborhood of $v$. The neighbor-locating chromatic number, denoted by $\chi_{\stackrel{}{NL}}(G)$, is the minimum cardinality of a neighbor-locating coloring of $G$. A $k$-coloring of a graph $G$ is a locating coloring if for each pair of vertices $\{u,v\}$ belonging to the same color $S_i$, there exists a color class $S_j$ such that $d(u,S_j)\neq d(v,S_j)$. The locating chromatic number, $\chi_{\stackrel{}{L}}(G)$, is the minimum cardinality of a locating coloring of $G$. In this paper we study these kinds of colorings for Moore cages.

Authors: Delia Garijo, Andrew Goodall and Anna De Mier
Apellidos: Garijo, Andrew Goodall and Anna De Mier
Title: Graph invariants from counting homomorphisms to Cayley graphs
abstract: The number of homomorphisms from a graph $G$ to graphs $G_q$ indexed by a positive integer $q$ defines an invariant of $G$. When $(G_q)$ is the sequence of complete graphs on $q$ vertices this invariant is the chromatic polynomial of $G$.  De la Harpe and Jaeger initiated the search for other sequences $(G_q)$ for which $\mathrm{hom}(G,G_q)$ is, for sufficiently large $q$, a polynomial in $q$ (depending only on $G$).  We extend this search to sequences for which $\mathrm{hom}(G,G_q)$ is a quasi-polynomial in $q$, or, more generally, for which the generating function $\sum_q \mathrm{hom}(G, G_q)z^q$ is rational. Our focus is on sequences $(G_q)$ arising from Cayley graphs; in particular, sequences of circulant graphs defined with reference to a fixed set of positive integers $D$, and the sequence of hypercubes.  For circulant graphs, after proving that it is necessary and sufficient for $D$ to be finite or cofinite for the sequence to yield a polynomial invariant,  we give a necessary condition on $D$ for the sequence to yield a quasi-polynomial invariant, and show that a quasi-polynomial indeed results when $D$ is an arithmetic progression. For hypercubes we show that the homomorphism numbers have a rational generating function.

Authors: Gabriela Araujo-Pardo, Julián Fresán-Figueroa, Linda Lesniak and Mika Olsen
Apellidos: Araujo-Pardo, Julián Fresán-Figueroa, Linda Lesniak and Mika Olsen
Title: Rainbow-free colorings of generalized quadrangles
abstract: We consider a generalized quadrangle as a hypergraph where its points and lines correspond to the vertices and hyperedges of the hypergraph, respectively. In a vertex coloring, a hyperedge is rainbow if all its vertices receive different colors. If no edge is rainbow, it is called a rainbow-free coloring. Given a hypergraph $H$, the maximum integer k such that there exists a rainbow-free $k$-coloring of $H$ is called the upper chromatic number, denoted by $\overline{\chi}(H)$.  We establish lower and upper bounds for the upper chromatic number of some generalized quadrangles and give the following exact values $\overline{\chi}(\mathcal{Q}(4,2))=6$ and $\overline{\chi}(\mathcal{Q}(4,3))=21$.

Authors: Patrick Morris, Eion Mulrenin, Maya Sankar and Liana Yepremyan
Apellidos: Morris, Eion Mulrenin, Maya Sankar and Liana Yepremyan
Title: Variants of Folkman numbers
abstract: The Folkman number $F(t)$ is the smallest possible  number of vertices of a graph $G$ which is $K_{t+1}$-free and  has the Ramsey property with respect to $K_t$, that is, any 2-colouring of the edges of $G$ results in a monochromatic $K_t$. Folkman numbers are at least as large as  Ramsey numbers, giving a lower bound of $F(t)\geq 2^{t/2}$.  On the other hand, the best known upper bound $F(t)\leq 2^{O(t^3)}$,  due to Balogh and Samotij, is achieved with a  random construction and the method of hypergraph containers. In this note, we introduce  off-diagonal and directed variants of Folkman numbers and obtain new bounds for these variants as well as for previously studied relaxed Folkman numbers. Our main focus is a pseudorandom clique-free graph construction   due to Bishnoi, Ihringer and Pepe  which is based on finite geometry.  We show that this explicit construction achieves a better upper bound for (certain parameters of) the Folkman number variants than the bounds obtained by adapting previous approaches for bounding classical Folkman numbers using random graphs.

Authors: Gordian Bruns, Dheer Noal Desai, Alexander Gavrilyuk, Josias Gomez and Nathan Lindzey
Apellidos: Bruns, Dheer Noal Desai, Alexander Gavrilyuk, Josias Gomez and Nathan Lindzey
Title: Intersecting Families of Spanning Trees of $K_{n,n}$
abstract: A family of spanning trees of a connected graph is $t$-intersecting if any pair of spanning trees in the family have $t$ or more edges in common. For sufficiently large $n$ and $t = O(n/\log n)$, we determine the extremal $t$-intersecting families of spanning trees in balanced complete bipartite graphs.

Authors: Yan Alves Radtke
Apellidos: Alves Radtke
Title: On the surjectivity of a map by Kapranov and Voevodsky in four dimensions
abstract: We consider a map defined by Kapranov and Voevodsky, mapping from the higher Bruhat orders to the higher Stasheff-Tamari orders on triangulations of the cyclic polytope. This map generalizes the quotient map given by the Silvester congruence, which is a map from  the weak Bruhat order on permutations to the Tamari lattice. It is an open question whether this map is surjective in all dimensions. The surjectivity for triangulations of two and three dimensional cyclic polytopes was proven by Thomas. We prove that the map is surjective in the four dimensional case.

Authors: Robin Simoens and Steven Van Overberghe
Apellidos: Simoens and Steven Van Overberghe
Title: An algorithm to find cospectral mates
abstract: We present an implementation of switching methods for the construction of graphs with the same spectrum. Our algorithm uses the canonical construction path method, which exploits the automorphism group of both the input graph and the switching method.

Authors: Thomas Lesgourgues, Michelle Delcourt and Luke Postle
Apellidos: Lesgourgues, Michelle Delcourt and Luke Postle
Title: Fractional Clique Decompositions of Dense Hypergraphs
abstract:
n 2014, Keevash famously proved the existence of $(n,q,r)$-Steiner systems as part of settling the Existence Conjecture of Combinatorial Designs (dating from the mid-1800s). In 2020, Glock, Kühn, and Osthus conjectured a minimum degree generalization: specifically that minimum $(r-1)$-degree at least $(1-\frac{C}{q^{r-1}})n$ suffices to guarantee that every sufficiently large $K_q^r$-divisible $r$-uniform hypergraph on $n$ vertices admits a $K_q^r$-decomposition (where $C$ is a constant that is allowed to depend on $r$ but not $q$).
The best-known progress on this conjecture is from the second proof of the Existence Conjecture by Glock, Kühn, Lo, and Osthus in 2016 who showed that $(1-\frac{C}{q^{2r}})n$ suffices. The fractional relaxation of the conjecture is crucial to improving the bound; for that, only the slightly better bound of $(1-\frac{C}{q^{2r-1}})n$ was known due to Barber, Kühn, Lo, Montgomery, and Osthus from 2017.
Our main result is to prove that $(1-\frac{C}{q^{r-1+o(1)}})n$ suffices for the fractional relaxation. Combined with the work of Henderson and Postle from 2025, this also shows that such $K_q^r$-divisible hypergraphs admit $K_q^r$-decompositions.

Authors: Sergio Cabello, Timothy M. Chan and Panos Giannopoulos
Apellidos: Cabello, Timothy M. Chan and Panos Giannopoulos
Title: Delaunay triangulations with predictions
abstract: We investigate algorithms with predictions in computational geometry, specifically focusing on the basic problem of computing 2D Delaunay triangulations. Given a set $P$ of $n$ points in the plane and a triangulation $G$ that serves as a "prediction" of the Delaunay triangulation, we would like to use $G$ to compute the correct Delaunay triangulation $\textit{DT}(P)$ more quickly when $G$ is "close" to $\textit{DT}(P)$. We obtain a variety of results under different deterministic and probabilistic settings.

Authors: Patrick Morris, Miquel Ortega and Juanjo Rue Juanjo Rue
Apellidos: Morris, Miquel Ortega and Juanjo Rue Juanjo Rue
Title: Counting subsets of integers with no arithmetic progression of a given length
abstract: Cameron and Erdős asked if  the number of subsets of $[n]$         that avoid arithmetic progressions of length $k$ is $2^{(1+o(1))         r_k(n)}$, where $r_k(n)$ is the maximum size of a $k$-AP-free subset of         $[n]$. Balogh, Liu, and Sharifzadeh made substantial progress towards         answering this question by showing that the number of such subsets is         $2^{O(r_k(n))}$ for some infinite sequence of integers $n$. We prove it         it is $2^{(1+o(1))r_k(n)}$ for $k \geq 5$ and an infinite sequence of         $n$, answering the question of Cameron and Erdős for these values.         This complements a previously announced result of the authors, which         shows that the bound $2^{O(r_k(n))}$ holds for all $n$ and $k \geq 3$.

Authors: Amanda Montejano, Luis Montejano and Oriol Serra
Apellidos: Montejano, Luis Montejano and Oriol Serra
Title: Discrete Brunn--Minkowski inequalities
abstract: The Brunn-Minkowski inequality is a fundamental result in convex geometry.  In the continuous setting, Bonnesen’s refinement incorporates $(d-1)$-dimensional projections, yielding sharper bounds that reflect geometric structure. A discrete counterpart is known only in dimension two, due to Grynkiewicz and Serra. In this paper, we explore extensions of this  result to higher dimensions by introducing a general framework to derive discrete Brunn--Minkowski-type inequalities.

Authors: Alejandro Galván, Jordi Castellví, Richard Coll, Clément Requilé and Juanjo Rué
Apellidos: Galván, Jordi Castellví, Richard Coll, Clément Requilé and Juanjo Rué
Title: Enumeration of maximal $K_5$ -minor free graphs
abstract: Building on Wagner's structural decomposition of maximal $K_5$-minor free labelled graphs in terms of clique-sums of some types of triangular graphs and the Wagner graph, we enumerate, both exactly and asymtpotically this class of graphs by means of generating functions and analytic combinatorics.  This opens the way to the study of the uniform random maximal $K_5$-minor free graph, and is the main motivation for this work.

Authors: Cristina Fernández-Córdoba, Sergi Sanchez Aragon and Mercè Villanueva
Apellidos: Fernández-Córdoba, Sergi Sanchez Aragon and Mercè Villanueva
Title: Hamming and homogeneous weight distributions of MacDonald codes over finite chain rings
abstract: A linear code of length $n$ over a finite chain ring $R$ with residue field $\mathbb{F}_q$ is an $R$-submodule of $R^n$. An $R$-linear code is a code over $\mathbb{F}_q$ (not necessarily linear) which is the Gray map image of a linear code over $R$. We present the construction of MacDonald codes over $R$ of type $\alpha$ and $\beta$, and their corresponding $R$-linear codes. Moreover, we show the fundamental parameters of these codes, as well as their Hamming and homogeneous weight distributions.

Authors: Daniel Camazón Portela and Juan Antonio Lopez Ramos
Apellidos: Camazón Portela and Juan Antonio Lopez Ramos
Title: Semigroup action based on skew polynomial evaluation with applications to Cryptography
abstract: Throughout this talk, we will introduce an action of the skew polynomial function right near-ring $\mathbb{F}_{q}\left[X; \sigma, \delta\right]$ on $\mathbb{F}_{q}$ based on its evaluation and the concept of the left skew product of functions. This will lead us to explore the construction of a certain subset $\mathcal{T}(X)\subset\mathbb{F}_{q}\left[X; \sigma, \delta\right]$, which allows us to control the non-commutativity of this ring, and exploit this fact to build a public key exchange protocol that is secure in the Canetti-Krawczyk model. The proposed protocol satisfies an interesting property; namely, even if an adversary $\mathcal{A}$ wins the game associated with the semigroup action problem with non-negligible probability with respect to a security parameter $\lambda$, this does not imply that $\mathcal{A}$ also wins the associated computational security game with non-negligible probability.

Authors: Pierre Aboulker, Charbit Pierre, Coulomb Samuel, Nurse Kathryn and Picasarri-Arrieta Lucas
Apellidos: Aboulker, Charbit Pierre, Coulomb Samuel, Nurse Kathryn and Picasarri-Arrieta Lucas
Title: Acyclic dichromatic number of tournaments:\\these are the champions
abstract: The acyclic dichromatic number of an oriented graph is the minimum size of a vertex-partition such that the digraphs induced by any single part are acyclic, and the oriented bipartite graphs between any two parts are acyclic too. We characterize the subtournaments that must appear in every tournament with sufficiently large acyclic dichromatic number, thereby confirming a conjecture of Bang-Jensen, Picasarri-Arrieta, and Yeo.

Authors: Filip Filipi
Apellidos: Filipi
Title: Generalized snarks for 2-vertex multigraphs with semi-edges
abstract: Motivated by topology, we say that a graph $A$ covers a connected graph $B$, if there exists a locally bijective graph homomorphism from $A$ onto $B$. In this context, graphs with multiple edges, loops, and semi-edges are considered. Recently, through the study of these covers, a notion generalizing snarks emerged. We say that a simple graph is an $(A,B)$-snark if it covers $A$ but does not cover $B$. It has been conjectured that if $A$, containing no semi-edges, does not cover $B$, then such an $(A,B)$-snark always exists. Several results on this conjecture have already been announced. In this extended abstract, we provide a complete characterization of the existence of $(A,B)$-snarks for $2$-vertex graphs $A,B$ in the general case, i.e., without forbidding semi-edges in $A$.

Authors: David Alcántara, Jette Gutzeit, Torben Donzelmann and Vincent Pilaud
Apellidos: Alcántara, Jette Gutzeit, Torben Donzelmann and Vincent Pilaud
Title: Shade Polytopes
abstract: The $j$-shadow of a binary tree is obtained by reading the sizes and positions of the subtrees along its $j$-th branch. We show that the fan obtained by coarsening the sylvester fan according to the fibers of the $j$-shadow map is the normal fan of a polytope, which is both a deformation and a removahedron of the associahedron (hence also of the permutahedron). Finally, we briefly discuss further topics related to shadow maps: shade lattices, the connection to pipe dreams, and shadow maps on some generalizations of binary trees.

Authors: Ralihe Raul Villagran Olivas
Apellidos: Villagran Olivas
Title: Sandpile groups of strongly regular graphs and the algebraic co-rank of twin-free graphs

Authors: Martina Juhnke
Apellidos: Juhnke
Title: Monotone paths on polytopes: Positive and negative results
abstract: To solve a linear program, the simplex method follows a path in the graph of a polytope, on which a linear function increases. The length of this path is a key measure of the complexity of the simplex method. Our starting point is a conjecture by Jesús De Loera stating that the number of paths counted according to their length forms a unimodal sequence. We give examples (old and new) for which this conjecture is true but we disprove this conjecture by constructing counterexamples for several classes of polytopes. However, we show that De Loera is “statistically correct”: We prove that the length of a coherent path on a random polytope (with vertices chosen uniformly on a sphere) admits a central limit theorem. This is joint work with Germain Poullot

Authors: Kolja Knauer
Apellidos: Knauer
Title: Endomorphisms of Sparse Graphs
abstract:
An endomorphism of a graph is a homomorphism from the graph to itself. Since endomorphisms are closed under composition, they form a monoid. It has been known since the 1960s that every finite monoid is isomorphic to the endomorphism monoid of some graph. This naturally led to the question of how restrictive a graph class $\mathcal G$ can be while still realizing every monoid (from a given class) as the endomorphism monoid of a graph in $\mathcal G$.
Particular attention has been devoted to sparse graph classes, such as planar graphs, minor-closed classes, and bounded-degree graphs. In this talk, I will survey some of the developments in this area and present two recent results that answer questions posed by Babai and Pultr in the 1980s:
1. Every commutative idempotent monoid (equivalently, every finite lattice) is isomorphic to the endomorphism monoid of a subcubic graph.
2. Every monoid is isomorphic to the endomorphism monoid of a regular graph.
Joint work with Gil Puig i Surroca.

Authors: Dimitrios Thilikos
Apellidos: Thilikos
Title: Deciding Asymptotic Properties of Minor-Closed Graph Classes
abstract: We examine the general problem of deciding properties of graph classes under the lens of quasi ordering relations. In particular, we consider properties of minor-closed graph classes whose finite descriptions are given by their obstruction sets. The question of whether such descriptions can be accompanied by polynomial-time decision algorithms can be viewed as a consequence of major questions in order theory. We present two instantiations of this general question for which such algorithms exist and can be explicitly constructed. In particular we examine asymptotic properties of minor-closed graph classes, such as growth constants and limiting densities.

Authors: Lisa Sauermann
Apellidos: Sauermann
Title: TBA
